CHRONIC KIDNEY DISEASE EDUCATION
Engage in daily physical activities, such as walking, and maintain control over blood pressure, blood
glucose, cholesterol levels, and weight.
Avoid alcohol, cigarette smoking, and NSAIDs like Advil and Aleve.
Limit dietary intake of high protein foods, saturated fats, phosphorus, potassium, and restrict sodium to
no more than 2 grams per day.
Opt for kidney-friendly foods including berries, grapes, cherries, apples, plums, cauliflower, onions,
eggplant, and turnips.
Choose lean proteins like poultry, fish, eggs, and unsalted seafood; drink water and unsweetened tea;
consume fish rich in omega-3 fatty acids.
(E11.51) Type 2 diabetes mellitus with peripheral vascular disease (CMS/HCC)
Managing your diabetes is crucial to prevent complications like nerve damage and poor blood flow.
Keep your blood sugar levels within the target range to reduce the risk of diabetic polyneuropathy.
Regular exercise and a healthy diet can help improve blood flow and manage peripheral vascular
disease.
Check your feet daily for any sores or changes, and report them to your doctor immediately.
Take your medications as prescribed and attend regular follow-up appointments to monitor your
condition.

Counseling for obesity :
Obesity is a complex disease involving an excessive amount of body fat. Obesity isn't just a cosmetic
concern.
It's a medical problem that increases the risk of other diseases and health problems, such as heart
disease, diabetes, high blood pressure and certain cancers.
The good news is that even modest weight loss can improve or prevent the health problems
associated with obesity.
A healthier diet, increased physical activity and behavior changes can help you lose weight.

Eating a healthy diet rich in fruits, vegetables, whole grains, and lean proteins helps maintain overall

Moderate exercise is known to reduce stress and anxiety. Aim for at least 30 minutes of moderate
exercise most days of the week.
Ensure 7-9 hours of quality sleep per night to help manage stress and reduce anxiety symptoms.
Reduce caffeine and sugar intake, as these can exacerbate anxiety symptom
Engaging in enjoyable activities and hobbies can provide a healthy distraction from stress and anxiety.

professional help.

EXAM: CT SOFT TISSUE NECK WITH CONTRAST
HISTORY: Nodule of the pharynx.
TECHNIQUE: Contiguous axial images were obtained utilizing a multislice, multidetector CT scanner. Post-
processed reformations were also submitted for review. The images were obtained following the administration
of IV contrast.
Contrast: 100 cc Optiray 350 IV contrast.
The total DLP was 671.31 mGy-cm and the CTDI was 20.48 mGy.
One or more of the following dose reduction techniques were used: automated exposure control, adjustment of
the mA and/or kV according to patient size, use of iterative reconstruction technique. A total of 1 CT
(Computed Tomography) examinations and 0 myocardial perfusion studies have been performed on this patient
over the past 12 months. Counts as indicated include examinations performed within our network.
COMPARISON: CT chest from 2/19/2026.
FINDINGS: Superficial right parotid calcification. Parotid and submandibular glands are otherwise normal.
Unremarkable appearance of the thyroid gland. 36 X 30 X 48 mm enhancing mass in the left oropharynx at the
base of the tongue with extension across the midline to the right. Mass abuts the epiglottis and left hyoid.
Centrally hypodense 27 X 35 X 38 mm right level 2A mass. Normal opacification of the neck vessels. Cervical
spondylosis with spinal canal and neural foraminal stenosis at C5-C6 and C6-C7. No aggressive osseous
lesions. No intracranial abnormality. Imaged paranasal sinuses, mastoid air cells, and orbits are unremarkable.
IMPRESSION:
Large left tongue base mass measuring 27 X 35 X 38 mm with extension across the midline to the right,
squamous cell carcinoma until proven otherwise.
Presumed metastatic right level 2A lymph node.
Cervical spondylosis.
Confidential
Patient:
Page 1 of 2

claude_vision.referral_reason: Urgent ENT consultation for a large left tongue base mass with extension across the midline, presumed squamous cell carcinoma, with associated presumed metastatic right level 2A lymph node. Patient also presented with hemoptysis (coughing up blood).
relevant_clinical_findings: Chief complaint: Follow-up and coughing up blood (hemoptysis)
CT neck with contrast revealed a large left tongue base mass measuring 27 x 35 x 38 mm with extension across the midline to the right — squamous cell carcinoma until proven otherwise
Mass abuts the epiglottis and left hyoid, raising concern for airway involvement
Centrally hypodense 27 x 35 x 38 mm right level 2A mass — presumed metastatic lymph node
36 x 30 x 48 mm enhancing mass in the left oropharynx at the base of the tongue noted on CT
Patient reports anxiousness and depressed mood (likely reactive to new diagnosis)
Allergy to Epinephrine — clinically relevant for any procedural planning
History of tonsillectomy as a child (surgical history relevant to ENT)
Physical exam: Oropharynx described as clear with no oropharyngeal exudate — mass not visualized on clinical exam, consistent with base of tongue/hypopharyngeal location
No cervical adenopathy noted on physical exam (despite CT showing level 2A node — discrepancy worth noting)
SpO2 96%, no respiratory distress noted at time of visit — airway currently intact but mass abuts epiglottis
ICD-10 diagnosis on referral: D37.02 — Neoplasm of uncertain behavior of base of tongue
Triage note from MD annotated on scheduling comments (handwritten: 'triage from MD')
claude_vision.imaging_summary: CT Soft Tissue Neck with Contrast performed at DRI Elk Grove (RadNet Imaging Center). FINDINGS: 36 x 30 x 48 mm enhancing mass in the left oropharynx at the base of the tongue with extension across the midline. Centrally hypodense 27 x 35 x 38 mm right level 2A mass consistent with presumed metastatic lymph node. Mass abuts the epiglottis and left hyoid. Superficial right parotid calcification. Cervical spondylosis with spinal canal and neural foraminal stenosis at C5-C6 and C6-C7. No aggressive osseous lesions. No intracranial abnormality. IMPRESSION: Large left tongue base mass measuring 27 x 35 x 38 mm with extension across the midline to the right — squamous cell carcinoma until proven otherwise. Presumed metastatic right level 2A lymph node. Cervical spondylosis. CT report IS included and attached (pages 13 of fax).

Date of birth fully legible confirmation — DOB is partially obscured across multiple pages
Prior ENT evaluation notes — no record of any prior ENT visit or head and neck specialist evaluation
Laryngoscopy or direct visualization results — no flexible or rigid scope findings documented despite mass abutting epiglottis
Smoking/tobacco history — not documented in social history (highly relevant for tongue base SCC risk stratification)
HPV status or HPV testing — not mentioned; critical for tongue base SCC prognosis and treatment planning
Referring provider direct contact number for expedited communication — only clinic main line and fax provided
Patient's preferred language / interpreter needs — not documented

provider_urgency_label.label: urgent
provider_urgency_label.source: Multiple locations: (1) Referral letter page 2 of fax states '**URGENT**' in bold above the diagnosis. (2) Referral Information table (page 4 of fax) lists 'Priority: Emergency' in the Priority field. (3) Status Reason field reads 'Care Ctr/Clinic Action Needed'. (4) Handwritten annotation on scheduling comments page 1 reads 'triage from MD' with an arrow. (5) Scheduling comments note on 03/10/2026 includes an attached document labeled 'URG'.

matched_criteria: Rapidly growing neck or oral lesions
evidence: CONFIRMED/SUSPECTED MALIGNANCY — CT impression states: 'Large left tongue base mass measuring 27 x 35 x 38 mm with extension across the midline to the right, squamous cell carcinoma until proven otherwise. Presumed metastatic right level 2A lymph node.' ICD-10 D37.02 assigned (Neoplasm of uncertain behavior of base of tongue).

RAPIDLY GROWING NECK/ORAL LESION — Mass measures 36 x 30 x 48 mm on imaging with extension across the midline and into the oropharynx; presumed nodal metastasis already present, indicating advanced local disease.

AIRWAY COMPROMISE RISK — CT findings note: 'Mass abuts the epiglottis and left hyoid,' indicating proximity to the airway with potential for progressive obstruction. Patient also presents with hemoptysis.
criteria_check.provider_label: Urgent — marked 'URGENT' in referral letter, 'Priority: Emergency' in referral form, with additional triage notation from MD and 'URG' attachment label.
criteria_check.reasoning: This referral is FLAGGED FOR PRIORITY REVIEW because the clinical content independently matches multiple urgent ENT criteria — suspected oropharyngeal malignancy with presumed nodal metastasis and a mass abutting the epiglottis — and the referring provider's urgency labels (Emergency/Urgent) are in full agreement with the clinical findings, making this a high-confidence priority case.

claude_vision.summary: This is an urgent ENT referral for a male patient with a large left tongue base mass (27–36 mm) identified on CT neck with contrast, described as squamous cell carcinoma until proven otherwise, with a presumed metastatic right level 2A lymph node. The mass abuts the epiglottis and left hyoid, raising concern for airway proximity, and the patient presented with hemoptysis. The referring provider marked this Emergency/Urgent, a concurrent oncology referral was submitted, and no tissue biopsy has yet been performed — making prompt ENT evaluation critical for tissue diagnosis, staging, and airway assessment.
